content:
Hi everyone,

I’m working on introducing Rust support in the Linux 5.10 kernel. As I understand, official Rust support was introduced in Linux 6.1. However, due to certain constraints, upgrading to a newer kernel version is not an option for us, so we are exploring the possibility of enabling Rust in 5.10.

I’d like to know if anyone has experience enabling Rust in the 5.10 kernel. Would backporting Rust support from 6.1 to 5.10 be feasible? What challenges should I expect, and how much effort would it take? Any insights or guidance would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks in advance!

Please see https://rust-for-linux.com/backporting-and-stable-lts-releases.

The Rust support in 6.1 was quite minimal, so even if you had access to that, would it be enough for what you need to do? i.e. it may be the case that you need/want features or code that are in later LTSs or perhaps mainline.

As for backporting to 5.10, if you mean doing the backport upstream, it is unlikely, although I cannot speak for the stable kernel team and their bandwidth/resources. If you mean maintaining a 5.10.y+Rust branch elsewhere by the Rust for Linux team, it is also a matter of bandwidth/resources and how many people would benefit from it. Either way (whether it is upstream or elsewhere), would your organization be willing to contribute somehow? Please feel free to contact me privately.

If you mean doing it yourself: it is technically feasible, but it is hard to say how much effort it would be -- it depends on your experience with the build system, the Rust toolchain, the Rust abstractions, and so on.

I hope that helps.

Qing Li said:

Hi everyone,

I’m working on introducing Rust support in the Linux 5.10 kernel. As I understand, official Rust support was introduced in Linux 6.1. However, due to certain constraints, upgrading to a newer kernel version is not an option for us, so we are exploring the possibility of enabling Rust in 5.10.

I’d like to know if anyone has experience enabling Rust in the 5.10 kernel. Would backporting Rust support from 6.1 to 5.10 be feasible? What challenges should I expect, and how much effort would it take? Any insights or guidance would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks in advance!

Currently, the Rust subsystem only includes bindings for some modules and a small number of C-side modifications, so supporting Rust in the 5.10 kernel should be feasible.

Hi @Miguel Ojeda, I currently also need to backport Rust support to the 5.10 kernel, as our company’s product is primarily based on the 5.10 kernel, and we have high requirements for system stability. We are conservative about updating the kernel version, and PCI & platform abstraction support starts from the 6.14 kernel.

I want to give it a try, as backporting to the 5.10 kernel might support me to write rust driver in production.

Miguel Ojeda said:

Please see https://rust-for-linux.com/backporting-and-stable-lts-releases.

The Rust support in 6.1 was quite minimal, so even if you had access to that, would it be enough for what you need to do? i.e. it may be the case that you need/want features or code that are in later LTSs or perhaps mainline.

As for backporting to 5.10, if you mean doing the backport upstream, it is unlikely, although I cannot speak for the stable kernel team and their bandwidth/resources. If you mean maintaining a 5.10.y+Rust branch elsewhere by the Rust for Linux team, it is also a matter of bandwidth/resources and how many people would benefit from it. Either way (whether it is upstream or elsewhere), would your organization be willing to contribute somehow? Please feel free to contact me privately.

If you mean doing it yourself: it is technically feasible, but it is hard to say how much effort it would be -- it depends on your experience with the build system, the Rust toolchain, the Rust abstractions, and so on.

I hope that helps.

Thanks for the detailed response!

I’ll give backporting a shot to see if I can get at least minimal Rust support, though I realize it might not fully meet our needs. I also checked out the link you shared, and I agree that for production, sticking with an LTS version maintained by the Rust for Linux team—like 6.6 LTS—would be the better way to go.

You're welcome, and sounds good, thanks!
By the way, a few days ago that page in our website had some extra sentences about 5.10 -- since it appears there may still be other interested folks, I will add back part of it.

JCKeep said:

Hi Miguel Ojeda, I currently also need to backport Rust support to the 5.10 kernel, as our company’s product is primarily based on the 5.10 kernel, and we have high requirements for system stability. We are conservative about updating the kernel version, and PCI & platform abstraction support starts from the 6.14 kernel.

I want to give it a try, as backporting to the 5.10 kernel might support me to write rust driver in production.

Thanks for chiming in!

If you end up with something that works for you and/or there are production users of an out-of-tree 5.10 backport and you are willing to maintain it and so on, then we can perhaps set up something for you all to collaborate, e.g. a repository or branch with several maintainers/companies involved

By the way, a few days ago that page in our website had some extra sentences about 5.10 -- since it appears there may still be other interested folks, I will add back part of it.

Done: https://rust-for-linux.com/backporting-and-stable-lts-releases
